targets.content: Maintain emission levels close to the world average of 7.58 MtCO2e by 2035
reduce its domestic GHG emissions of 10% by 2030 as compared to a no-policy scenario of 2015 to 2030, with an intermediary indicative mitigation target of 5% reduction by 2025
The forthcoming LT-LEDS (2050) will operationalise Sierra Leone’s net-zero vision.

Reduction of 214 GgCO₂ per year by the introduction of 100 electric buses; by 2035
Related 2030 targets:
- 50 e-buses in service; ≥7,500 pax/day; (Ind.: fleet registry; AFC/ticketing data)
- Availability ≥ 90%; energy use ≤ 1.2–1.5 kWh/km (route-dependent); (Ind.: telematics)
- 2 depots electrified; 10 chargers installed; (Ind.: charger uptime logs)
- 107 ktCO₂e/yr avoided (net of grid emissions); (Ind.: tCO₂e/yr)

Related 2035 targets:
- 100 e-buses; 15,000 pax/day; (Ind.: AFC; AVL/telematics)
- Availability ≥ 92%; energy use ≤ 1.1–1.4 kWh/km; (Ind.: telematics)
- 4 depots; 20 chargers; (Ind.: uptime logs)
- 214 ktCO₂e/yr avoided; (Ind.: tCO₂e/yr)

Reduction of 141.5 GgCO₂ per year by the introduction of 10,000 electric kekehs; by 2035
Related 2030 targets: 
- 5,000 e-kekeh in operation, around 35% of kekehs; (Ind.: registration data; vendor sales)
- Average energy use ≤ 35–45 Wh/km; availability ≥ 92%; (Ind.: telematics)
- 150 neighbourhood charge points; 25 swap kiosks; (Ind.: uptime logs)
- 67.5 ktCO₂e/yr avoided (net of grid emissions); (Ind.: tCO₂e/yr)

Related 2035 targets:
- 10,000 e-kekeh, around 70%; (Ind.: registrations; telematics)
- Energy use ≤ 35–45 Wh/km; availability ≥ 94%; (Ind.: telematics)
- 300 charge points; 50 swap kiosks; (Ind.: uptime logs)
- 141.5 ktCO₂e/yr avoided; (Ind.: tCO₂e/yr)

Reduction of 179 GgCO₂ per year by improving the efficiency of the vehicle fleet; by 2035
Related 2030 targets:
- Import standards gazetted; max age 10 years (LDV); inspection coverage ≥ 70%; (Ind.: legal notices; inspection stats)
- Average fuel intensity reduced 10% vs 2025 baseline; (Ind.: cohort model; inspection data)
- 20–35 million litres/yr saved (range to be refined); (Ind.: fuel sales vs baseline; model outputs)
- 77 ktCO₂e/yr avoided; (Ind.: tCO₂e/yr)
- Road‑worthiness failure rate declines 30%; sulphur/spec standards enforced; (Ind.: inspection logs; fuel QA)

Related 2035 targets:
- Max age 8 years; inspection coverage ≥ 90%; (Ind.: legal notices; inspection stats)
- Average fuel intensity reduced 20%; (Ind.: cohort model)
- 45–70 million litres/yr saved; (Ind.: same)
- 179 ktCO₂e/yr avoided; (Ind.: tCO₂e/yr)
- Failure rate declines 50%; (Ind.: inspection logs)
Reduce GHG emissions from the transport sector by 16.2% below BAU levels by 2035 [251.5 Gg CO2 per year in 2030 → 534.5 Gg CO2 in 2035]

measures.quote: Diversify economic growth through strengthened transport sub-sector (particularly infrastructure) to reduce regional and global emissions and build a stable economy.
Improve and promote use of public transport (e.g. road, rail and water) for passengers and cargo to reduce traffic congestion and GHG emissions

measures.instrument: Freight transport shifting to rail or inland waterways
measures.quote: Improve and promote use of public transport (e.g. road, rail and water) for passengers and cargo to reduce traffic congestion and GHG emissions

measures.instrument: General transport and land use planning
measures.quote: Formulate transport plans
Develop and enforce regulations on regular maintenance of vehicles and vehicle emission testing
Mass transportation (rail, road and water) for passengers and cargoes using clean alternative energy sources
Improvement of the water transport system
Quality control for spare parts for all types of vehicles
Promote emission testing for all heavy types of machinery and vehicles
Development of transport infrastructure to incorporate walkways and bicycle tracks
Development of transport infrastructure to incorporate walkways and bicycle tracks

Introduce E-mobility programme and plans to promote the use of electric buses, passenger vehicles (cars) and motor tricycles, resulting in displaced conventional vehicle sales, transport fuel imports and associated GHG emissions.

measures.instrument: Vehicle restrictions (import, age, access, sale, taxation)
measures.quote: Develop strategy on the age limit of vehicles imported into Sierra Leone

The National e-Mobility Strategy and Implementation Plan, the GEF/UNEP-supported “Supporting Sierra Leone with the Shift to Electric Mobility” project, and pilot efforts such as the EPA-led e-kekeh demonstration, the NEEV Salone e-vehicle assembly, and UNDP-backed commercial EV feasibility studies have collectively established the institutional, technical, and policy groundwork for electric transport.
The e-bus program extends this transition by introducing battery-electric buses on high-demand urban routes in Freetown and regional hubs.

The first phase prioritises depot-based overnight charging and opportunity charging at terminals, using 9–12 m buses for trunk routes and 6–8 m models for feeders, all equipped with regenerative braking, telematics, and CCS2 chargers.
Complementary investments include route redesign, depot electrification (transformers and 60–180 kW chargers), driver and mechanic training, and private-operator service contracts.
Complementary investments include route redesign, depot electrification (transformers and 60–180 kW chargers), driver and mechanic training, and private-operator service contracts.

Integration with renewable PPAs and time-of-use tariffs ensures a low-carbon power supply and reduced operating costs, directly linking the transport and energy sectors through findings from the RE-Based EV Charging report.

Over the past two years, the country has successfully piloted electric three-wheelers and developed a Comprehensive Implementation Plan that includes vehicle procurement, the installation of solar-powered charging and battery-swapping stations, and the collection of operational and social data. These pilots have confirmed the technical and economic viability of electric mobility in urban contexts, while demonstrating co-benefits such as improved air quality, lower transport costs, and enhanced gender inclusion—reflected in the target of 30% female drivers.

The lessons learned from these early efforts have helped to establish a replicable framework for scaling electric mobility in Sierra Leone’s transport sector and linking it with renewable energy deployment.

Financing mechanisms such as leasing, pay-as-you-go, and concessional loans will make vehicle ownership more accessible to drivers and cooperatives, while integration into national transport planning will align e-CMV operations with e-bus and low-carbon public transport corridors. The deployment model—combining driver cooperatives and fleet-as-a-service arrangements—ensures affordability and operational efficiency, supported by clear vendor qualification, safety, and regulatory oversight from MoTA, FCC, and SLRSA.

measures.instrument: General transport and land use planning
measures.quote: This vehicle efficiency and emissions measure builds on Sierra Leone’s earlier policy frameworks, particularly the National Energy Efficiency Action Plan (2015) and the National e-Mobility Strategy and Implementation Plan, which together laid the groundwork for improving transport-sector energy performance and aligning it with the country’s clean mobility goals.

The proposed measure seeks to translate policy into action by enforcing fuel economy, age, and emissions standards for vehicles, thereby complementing the ongoing e-mobility and renewable transport initiatives.

measures.instrument: Vehicle restrictions (import, age, access, sale, taxation)
measures.quote: It introduces phased age and emission limits—starting with a 10–12-year import cap for light-duty vehicles and tightening to 5–7 years by 2035
minimum fuel-efficiency threshold
mandatory inspection protocols for emissions and onboard diagnostics (OBD)
Implementation will be supported by a digital vehicle registry capturing model year, fuel type, and odometer readings to improve fleet monitoring and policy planning.
Enabling activities include enforcing labelling and inspection standards, aligning fuel-efficiency regulations with the e-mobility roadmap
introducing fiscal incentives and green procurement policies for cleaner fleets.

adaptation.quote: Designing and improvement of provincial and feeder roads using Climate resilient surfacing materials.
Support the construction of appropriate roads particularly feeder roads in the rural areas as a climate resilience strategy

Support the implementation of infrastructure that fosters the development of electric buses and electric Kekehs.
o Ensure the low-income population reaches jobs, education and healthcare services, improving their access to economic and social opportunities.
o Ensure climate-resilient transport systems, improving safe and equitable access to schools, health, and protection services.

Climate-Proofing Roads and Bridges
Detailed Description
This aligns well with national infrastructure priorities, and it is linked to the National Road Maintenance
Strategy, SLRA’s climate risk screening tool, and the World Bank’s Transport Infrastructure Resilience
Project.
Upgrade resilient design codes, design standards, and rehabilitate priority corridors with improved
drainage, higher bridge clearances, scour protection, and climate-resilient materials. Introduce asset
management systems that integrate climate risk and lifecycle maintenance budgeting.
Upgrade resilient design codes, design standards,
